tracker_summary.inventor: Alexander Fleming (discovery), Howard Florey, Ernst Chain, and Norman Heatley (therapeutic development)
tracker_summary.date: Discovery: September 1928; First therapeutic use: 1941; Mass production: 1943–1944
tracker_summary.current_use: Penicillin and derivatives remain among the most widely prescribed antibiotics globally; annual antibiotic market exceeds $40 billion
tracker_summary.initial_funding_source: British War Office / Medical Research Committee — World War I wound infection research
tracker_summary.initial_funding_reason: Catastrophic mortality from infected battlefield wounds in WWI

the_x: British War Office funding for WWI wound-infection bacteriology research at St. Mary's Hospital, London
the_y: The world's first mass-produced antibiotic, saving an estimated 200 million lives in the 20th century
summary: The story of penicillin is not one flash of genius but a 16-year chain of accidents, neglect, wartime desperation, and transatlantic collaboration. Fleming's 1928 contaminated petri dish was real but unactionable — he couldn't purify the compound, and the medical establishment ignored his paper for a decade. It restarted only when Florey and Chain at Oxford, funded by the Rockefeller Foundation, picked it up in 1939. Their first patient nearly died when they ran out of drug. Florey flew to America with mold in his coat lining. A lab assistant found the highest-yielding mold in history on a Peoria cantaloupe. By D-Day, enough penicillin existed to treat every Allied wounded soldier.

chain.where: Western Front; St. Mary's Hospital, London
chain.what_they_were_doing: Watched antiseptics kill white blood cells faster than bacteria in infected wounds.
chain.funding: British War Office; Medical Research Committee.

chain.what_they_were_doing: Returned from vacation; Penicillium mold had drifted onto a staph dish, leaving a clear kill zone.
chain.the_jump: Fleming couldn't purify or stabilize it. His 1929 paper was largely ignored; he stopped pursuing it by 1931.
chain.funding: Medical Research Council; St. Mary's Hospital.

chain.who: Howard Florey, Ernst Chain, Norman Heatley
chain.where: Sir William Dunn School of Pathology, Oxford
chain.what_they_were_doing: Found Fleming's paper in a literature search; Heatley built extraction apparatus from milk churns.
chain.the_jump: May 25, 1940: all untreated mice died; all penicillin-treated mice survived.

chain.what_they_were_doing: First human patient's fever broke in 24 hours — then they ran out of drug, recycled penicillin from his urine, and he died. Florey flew to America with mold spores rubbed into his coat lining.
chain.the_jump: NEAR-DEATH MOMENT: Alexander's death proved the drug worked but Oxford's micro-batch production was wholly inadequate. Only American industrial scale could save it.
chain.funding: MRC emergency wartime grant; Rockefeller Foundation; US OSRD.

chain.what_they_were_doing: Found a cantaloupe at a local market yielding 200x more penicillin than Fleming's strain.
chain.the_jump: After mutagenesis, 1,000x yield. Virtually all penicillin today descends from that cantaloupe.

chain.what_they_were_doing: Pfizer converted a Brooklyn ice plant into a fermentation facility under War Production Board contracts.
chain.the_jump: By June 1944: 650 billion units per month. By D-Day, enough to treat every wounded Allied soldier.
chain.funding: War Production Board guaranteed procurement; US Army Surgeon General.

sources: Fleming A (1929). British J Experimental Pathology, 10(3):226–236. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2048009/
Chain E, Florey HW, et al. (1940). The Lancet, 236(6104):226–228. https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140-6736(01)08728-1/fulltext
Lax E (2004). The Mold in Dr. Florey's Coat. Henry Holt.
ACS (1999). Discovery and Development of Penicillin. https://www.acs.org/content/acs/en/education/whatischemistry/landmarks/flemingpenicillin.html
NLM/NIH. 'The Discovery and Development of Penicillin 1928–1945.' https://www.nlm.nih.gov/exhibition/penicillin/index.html
Neushul P (1993). J History of Medicine, 48(4):371–395. https://doi.org/10.1093/jhmas/48.4.371
Hafner K, Lyon M. Where Wizards Stay Up Late. Simon & Schuster, 1996.
